Hey guys, JB is in the house for the weekly intensive. I have a great topic. So I have a burning desire. I wanna talk to you guys about. So what's the interesting thing is I'm training my own sales staff now. I finally have some time to even direct that. With those busy, you know, putting the finishing touches on certification for the last six months. I mean, it was done like six months ago. It took me that much time to like, you know, we shoot certain scenes and bring it up to, you know, different levels and blah, blah, blah, anyway. So I really had a chance to listen to my own people, you know, and their calls and stuff. And it's really, it's crystallized something in my own belief now about how I should teach the straight line system. And I wouldn't say that, not that I'm just making a mistake with it, that I never make mistakes, no. It's not that, but I think I understand one thing that I, that one reason why I keep seeing the same sort of mistake being made again and again and again. And it's interesting cause it's, you know, once you get to a certain level of proficiency, right? I say, well, you can close anyone who's closeable. That mistake vanishes. But my goal here is to try to collapse the amount of time it takes from when you first start using the system to when you get to that, like that says a weird cross over all of a sudden, it's like, oh, and you become like elegant and you see your clothes very flies up, right? And, you know, I'm noticing it with like people all over the world now. I'm getting, you know, I get, you guys write me. So, you know, so that's why I guess the reason like, you know, I talk about the good enough factor, it's all true. Like it all, it all makes sense. But at the end, yeah, because once you hit that tipping point, you automatically stop making this mistake. And that's my goal for tonight, to get you to this point where you stop making this one classic mistake. And that is, it's not fully understanding what the boundaries above and below the line really mean. And then so I'll take responsibility for this, I will. And the fact that maybe I didn't explain this as thoroughly as I should have, maybe I just took it for granted people understood, right? So I wanna kinda slow this aspect of the sale down. I'm gonna draw it out for you on the board here. But I wanna really slow down this one part of the sale in terms of this whole idea of taking control, gathering intelligence, not going off to Pluto, being a better listener than just a talker, but yet still getting your progress. What's really happening with all this, right? There's a certain sweet spot that occurs in a well-administered straight line sale, whereby you are essentially running these scripted patterns, but in a completely natural way. In other words, there's nothing wooden or inauthentic about it. So while you're actually reading from a script, so to speak, like the actual words are these chunks of information that you're getting out there, right? They're scripted little individual bite-sized chunks. Imagine this, right? Imagine if like you had, like there's like, you know, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom. Like there's always, each one of these, each of these is a nugget of information. It's a well-constructed nugget of information. Like for example, a couple of your symbols, listen Jim, you know, hey, Jordan Belville calling from X, Y, Z company in New York, how you doing today? That's a nugget of information, that's a nugget. So the idea goes, that Jim reasoned for the call, said something just came up, that's a nugget of information. Great, okay, Jim, we're one of the most well-respected people in the entire X, Y, Z industry, that's a nugget, right? Jim, let me just ask you a couple quick questions so I don't, that's a nugget. So you have all these different nuggets of information, right? That have to get across to the first, let's say 10 to 20 seconds, who you are, why you're calling, what the calls are in reference to. You need to ask them some questions so you can best serve them, right? All this stuff has to come across. And then you have these, let's say one through eight, or your intelligence gathering questions. Each one of those is a nugget, right? What I mean by nugget is, what kind of work you're doing now? How long you living in the area for? What kind of system are you using? There's based on what you're selling, there's gonna be a mixture of questions that are somewhat very specific to your industry. They're only for your industry. Others are like general big picture question, you know, what, tell me if you've, you know, in the past, what kind of other products have you used like this, what you like most about it, what you like least about it? For those of you who have been through certification, you know exactly what I'm talking about. Where you have this mixture of specific questions directly that are for your business and industry, those are just very generic big picture questions. So what you're doing is, is you're essentially figuring out, you know, what's the most empowering order to ask those questions. Each question I'm talking about is a nugget. Something you have to ask, something that's gonna come out, and then based on that, right, you keep moving down the line. So it's all of those nuggets form the front end of the presentation, right, up to the point where you, and by the way, this could be including, you know, if you really wanna take it one step further, it even includes the actual presentation itself before you ask for the order. But I don't wanna confuse things. I wanna, I just want you to understand what's going on here. In other words, before you pick up the phone, before you meet anybody face to face, there's all these sort of required things that have to come out in the conversation to influence, to persuade who you are, where you're calling from, the purpose of the call. You know, let me tell you who we are, what we do with a number one, this is all boom, boom, all the beginning, right? So what we do with the straight line is we construct this perfect script. And that script represents the best 10 to 15 presentations we've ever had all wrapped up into one perfect one. When did we sound best? You know, hey Bill, how you doing today? Listen, Bill, you might not have heard of my company XYZ with the number one, this into that industry, and Jim, you know, all we're gonna do right now is take your suspicious, whatever you're selling, there's like this one pitch that represents the perfect, like that cuts through all of this stuff, right? And sort of consolidates it into one perfect presentation. That's the goal of the straight line. So now watch, and I'm leading up to some really profound here as you're about to see. And it really thinks you're changing, it really changes people's lives here, especially the new people. And for those who are teaching this to your staff, it's gonna give you a greater understanding. So when I teach the straight line, here's the way I've always taught it in the past to you. I say, watch, I say now, you know, I say every sale is the same, right? And I drew this long, what the fuck happened over here, guys? You gotta make me a better, you gotta have better storage on these things, right? Okay, so, okay, I drew this long thin straight line across the center of the board. I put a big thick X on either end. This is your open where the sale begins. This is your close where the process is, yeah, let's do it, right? And then what I say is that every once in a while, you get one of those perfect straight line sales where everything you say, everything you do, the prospect says yes, yes, yes, yes, yes, yes, yes, all the way down to the time when you ask for the order for the first time and he says, yeah, great, let's do it. And he, and here she buys. That's the perfect straight line sale, right? I say, well, the problem with that is that in the real world, these types of sales are few and fall between. What typically happens is your prospect tries to take you off the straight line. They have questions, they have concerns, they have objections, they wanna know more, whatever it might be, right? So what we have are these healthy boundaries above and below the line. When you're inside these boundaries, you're essentially in control of the sale. And when you cross outside these boundaries, you're out of control, out of control. I mean, the prospect is in control, right? And then I say, and the example I use is like, for instance, you say to someone, so Jim, tell me, how long are you living in the area for? Let's say that was one of your first intelligence gathering questions. So you've already been through this opening and you've asked permission to ask questions. You think, so tell me, Jim, how long are you living in the area? And then the prospect starts to respond. They say, I'm living in the area for six months and before that I lived over in Richmond, making the words up, right? And that's all good. But then sometimes they'll say, yeah, and I moved over as I like to go duck hunting and they start talking about ducks. And you're like, oh my God, duck hunting, I love duck hunting. And you start talking about ducks too, because you think that if you talk about ducks, then that will get you through a poor. So you spend 10 minutes up here rambling on about the merits or demerits of going and hunting a bunch of innocent ducks and shooting them and cooking them. No judgment there either way. But the point is, and I call that process going off the Pluto. Right, so you're Pluto up here and here you have your anus, right? And everyone answers your anus, not a good place to be. So the way I always describe going off the Pluto is that it typically happens when your prospect is asked a question and they'll answer it and there's something in the answer where they'll start off answering the question in a way that is germane. Meaning they'll give you the information you need to see if they qualify and what their needs are. But somehow they got lost in their own thoughts and they start talking about things that are non-relevant and they start rambling. And while that into itself is not a problem, it's actually very common, the problem starts when rather than listening and saying, oh wow, nice, oh my God, cool, uh-huh, got it, got it, got it, you, and then taking them back into the sale, what you do is you actually, oh my God, duck hunting, I love duck hunting, and you start making the conversation about that. That's how I define going off the Pluto, right? So from that perspective, what do these boundaries really represent? They represent essentially how far you can stray off of this perfect presentation that you've created in your own mind. In other words, remember, what do we do with the straight line? It's about strategic preparation. Since we know, now I'm gonna get a little advanced here, so for all you beginners here, actually you can follow what I'm saying here, okay? If you're very profound, I'm about to say here. So we know that we have, there's five core elements of the straight line. And of those five, three are a choir elements. They must love the product, trust you, trust the company that stands behind the product, the three tens, right? And then the higher level, those, these other two are the action threshold and pain threshold. And the best way to describe this is like, in other words, for easy buyers and easier sales, the first three numbers of the person's combination might be enough. If you just manipulate and address these three, they'll buy. If one of these is not lined up, I don't care who they are, they're not gonna buy. People don't buy when they hate what the product is, but don't trust the salesman, but don't trust, they won't buy from you. These three are sort of required numbers. These two are the ones that come on if the first three don't work. So the metaphor would be, so you have certain safes like a master combination, like they come in used in a gym locker in school, you use three numbers, bam, bam, bam, yeah, great. Well, if you're gonna have a million dollars in a safe, probably guess what? Ain't gonna be three numbers. They make the safe more robust. They add on additional numbers. Those safes are harder to open. You can't crack that number as quickly as a three digit code. So a three digit code has some level of security before it'll open, five digit code has more, right? So, and again, in the same way they just recently like actually added on numbers to our phone, at least my phone, it's like now six digits. It used to be four digits to my password. They add on two, makes it more difficult for someone to crack. If you look at sales like that, you're lining up, you're cracking the code, cracking the combination, right? So these first three are acquired numbers. You're gonna address them every single time. But when you get to easy sales, like people like me were easy to sell to and it's a low priced item, a kind of an impulse decision, you might never have to address these on the numbers, but you're always gonna have to address these three, right? So with the straight line, what we're doing is say, okay, wait, you know, we go to the back first, okay, great, I know that if I wanna get someone to say, yes, that's my outcome, I want someone, yeah, I wanna buy it. Well, I know that I'm gonna have to get these three numbers at some point along this line lined up. I gotta get to the very high levels of certainty. They don't, they're not gonna buy, right? So where are they when they enter the encounter? Well, they're at lower levels, they don't know yet, they don't know all the things you know. They're not sure how great the process, they haven't seen it, heard it, read it, read all about the benefits and features, you get it, right? So you educate them, you say things, you do things, you send them things, you show them demonstrations, show them testimonials, all design, all of it, to increase the certainty for these three numbers. That's what you're doing. Making them absolutely certain they love the product, trust to connect with you, trust to connect with the company, both logically and emotionally. And again, then we ask for the order if they're all lined up and is it easier sale with an easier person in terms of their action first off the line, right there. But what we're doing is, so we give ourselves this edge by saying, ah, I know that these three numbers have to line up. So I go to the end, I say, right, looking back to the front, what's the most elegant way? Like, let me prepare myself, let me not wing this. Like, what's gonna be the best way for me to line up those numbers? What should I say first? What should I say second? Well, I've spent 30 years now perfecting that. That's what the straight line is. And I had perfected enough that very night I had invented it back in 1988 to essentially make that first evolution of the straight line where I was able to essentially hand my guys that evening a syntax, a template, the order in which you go about lining up these elements. That's what I created the first night. An order in which you go about lining up these three core elements that are necessary to do that come after it, right? The first straight line and the word we use with syntax, watch, it's the words, fancy words, right? Okay, tell Travis he's in trouble for this. He can never, ever give you a bumper bump, okay? Like I need to uninstall it. Okay, watch, because I really want to write on like this. So watch, the word syntax means order. So fancy words for order. The order in which you go about influencing. So I said the first step of the syntax is what? That we must take control. We must take control of the sale. Because why? Without control, you can't run any patterns. You can't line anything up because they're directing what goes first. What's the point of trying to, okay, I'm gonna take these 10 steps, but someone else is telling you what step comes first. This is point, it's an oxymoron. Nothing can happen until you're in control of the process. Right? So as it turns out, the way we take control is we leverage off of just the great truth. It's a truth about human beings and communication that when we are in the presence of people that we deem to be experts, what do we do? We allow them to control. We say, okay, you're an expert, great. You've earned the right. Ask what you want, because I'll answer you because you're an expert. You're educated, you've done the work. You've earned the right for me to say, okay, go ahead, I defer. When you're in the position of presence of a novice, what do you do? You say, I'm not gonna defer. I'll take control. This first step and how you actually do it through the use of tonality, body language, certain key patterns, right? This is enough to allow people to have that good enough factor with the straight line. We're like a Maxwell, John Massilton, where they just get this instant pop of 30 plus percent on sales just because of this, just because of the awareness of the sales force and I said, oh my God, if I sound a certain way, if I just greet someone the right way, if I show them enthusiastic, sound like an expert, use certain tonalities, people say, whoa, this person actually sounds pretty damn good. I will defer to them. And from that alone, you get an increase, it's crazy, but it works. You get an increase in your closing rate, not just because of that you took control. It's what that control allow you to do and opened up the possibility for a productive conversation. In the absence of that, there's no possibility. You're like the outmatched price fired, you're covering up from, you're playing essentially reactive versus proactive ball. It's a game, you're playing the game of being reactive versus proactive. Proactive is what we want to be. We want to control where we're going. We have a destination. We know we get along, those elements are proactive. So this first step was taking control. And that was a monumental leap that night back in 88 when I realized why am my guys just simply unable to close rich people? Why am I having no trouble doing it? Why is Danny not having trouble? The reason was, I was a natural ball closer. I was highly educated and I sounded a certain way and I had Danny to do the same. And he was highly educated natural ball closer. And that's one of the advantages natural ball closers have. They have this way, this ability to naturally, for whatever reason, their brain is white. They sound good. They sound like experts. And that opens up the possibility now to move the sale along whatever track you deem to be most productive. Now, that's why you have natural ball closers. They're okay and they do well. And there's people like me who are like the highest level where I then have all the other stuff figured out behind that. So there's like this natural ball closers and this natural ball closers. I did a podcast with a great, and I don't want to tell you who's great top ball player, Barnes is the last name. You'll see it next week, right? And he's unbelievable. He said there's one Federer, there's one Poby, there's one Michael, and hey, I'm great, but there's a certain level, right? So even the same thing goes to sales. So there's many natural ball closers, not a lot of guys like me who have figured out the whole entire package from start to finish. But the point I'm making here is that once you have control, it opens up the possibility to now move the sale a certain way. That script represents your best guess, your best shot of what the perfect sale is gonna sound like, what the perfect sales is gonna sound like. What is the best opening words that you could say? All things, it's like the sum average of everything, right? It represents the sum average of what's the best order of my intelligence gathering questions. It represents the best average of what that transition, what that solutions, right? It's like your template, right? And that is your straight line presentation. But guess what? Within these boundaries is an infinite number of variations of that. That are all inside the boundaries mean they're correct, they're effective, they're not spotless, but they're not exactly what you wrote down in your script. You're not going word for word, question for question. In other words, sometimes you will, hopefully very often you will. You get this sort of generic type of client that, and this is, your script is designed for like the most common type of person who goes through your funnel and that is gonna be the perfect presentation for that person, but guess what? For everyone like that, there's gonna be five who aren't. You're not gonna have the perfect presentation for everyone. So what you have are these minor variations of your script that are inside the boundaries and you have to free form. Meaning when you get on the phone with, so let's say, let me give it to you this way. Let's say that in order for someone, let me just take a frozen grip of my mouth, right? Let's say you're selling a product and in order for someone to be financially qualified, they have to be liquid for $300 and be able to make payments for six months or a year, whatever that might be, right? Well, in the average presentation, there might be two or three questions that sort of deal with that, but if you are in a situation when you ask someone a question, the first question, and in that response, it becomes painfully clear they have more than enough money to afford your product. They know a lot about your product. In other words, if they, if when you are in the real world of sales, you actually listen to what the prospect says, you're speaking to human beings and based on what they say, you change the script so it sounds logical, and fluid or else your Siri or Alexa from Amazon where you ask them a question. Siri, what's the weather out today? Oh, right now the weather is 74 degrees. Great, and Siri, and you think I should use a raincoat? Oh, we can Google information on raincoats. They can't connect two questions together. That's what you act like when you're in novice with a straight line. The prospect says something to you, but you don't use that to impact what you're going to say next. Your script is a model. It's the model for the perfect sale. You have boundaries that run not just for when they scoff the pool. Like that meaning it's you, you have the poetic license to move around and wiggle around to be elegant. So you don't sound like a moron when you're on the phone. So when someone who's sophisticated, smart, that knows about your product, you're not saying to them, so tell me what's your greatest pain for, what the, are you talking about? What are you talking about? Like I already answered that implicitly with my first quite answer to you, I told you all about myself. I listened to it in a call. What made me realize is my area, I was already leading up to this because I went through this with Maxwell, the team, where they hit this great illumination about what really happens in the sales. And it's something I always do and teach, but when you see it happen with a company, like, oh my God, it's so obvious, right? In other words, the script represents your best shot. It has all the questions you would need to answer, but very or ask to get the information you need to sell. But very often, one or two questions resolves everything, because the answer they give you ticks three or four boxes, or you can use deductive reasons as they're qualified. Once you know someone's financially qualified, don't ask any more financial qualification questions. What do I always say to you? The rule, or they say, how many questions that I ask? Here's the answer, and I'll say it again to you, not one more than is necessary, but not one less. You ask questions only because they're necessary. You don't ask questions for the sake of asking questions. Why would you say this one? So what's your closing rate right now? Do you need, how does that help you close a sale? In other words, if you ask someone a specific question, like what percentage of people do you close? I don't need to know that. In other words, if I'm trying to sell someone a training system for sales, I wanna know what type of work you're doing, okay, are you a business owner, are you a sales manager, how long you're doing it for, how are things going, what are you struggling with? Okay, how much you know about the straight line system? I'm not gonna ask you deeper and deeper questions about things just because I wanna, because in my script, for someone that's a complete novice that knows nothing about the straight line, I didn't say, so how much do you know about the straight line system? When the person first said, oh my God, I love Jordan, I watch all the stuff. So I'm not gonna say, what do you know about the straight line? I modify it or so, have you bought any specific programs in the past, or you just know it from online or read the books? You gotta be smart. Stop acting like Siri and start really listening to what you say, what you say to someone, how they respond and based on how they respond, what they need to hear to maintain this feeling, yep, this person is an expert, you're moving down the line and the boundaries represent, this is your poetic license. Perfect example, go to the mock sale I did for the dairy industry and watch what the straight line really looks like when it's deployed in a sale. And I do it, I am not following a verbatim script, but I'm in front of the things I'm saying are all from my script and yeah, every one set of every seven pitches, I'll almost be directly on the line because that's what that particular person called for. I don't try, you guys are trying to shove people into the straight line, that's not the way this works. The boundaries represent your wiggle room to adopt the straight line to them, not the other way around. Does that make sense? This is the holy grail and the reason why this vanishes when you close everyone, you hit all of them, oh, I get it because when you become unconsciously competent, your unconscious mind realizes this automatically and it reorders the way you use the straight line. That's why you have this, people get this instant, they increase their clothes, and all of a sudden I get this, oh my God, it hit me and wham, and I guarantee if I listen to that person, they're not using the same script every time, but the script is in front of them, they use it here, they'll wiggle around, they'll go back, they dance around the script, and these boundaries above and below the line is what gives you that essentially, call it a poetic license or wiggle room, to adapt the straight line so it fits seamlessly into whatever you're selling and more importantly, to the person that you're speaking to. The worst thing that should ever happen is when your prospect is literally moving you down the straight line and you're still playing catch up ball, trying to ask intelligence gathering questions, because they want to be like, so tell me what you got? I'm like, well first, a couple, you adapt, so when someone's rushing you, what do you do? You start presenting and qualifying, go to the milk thing. So if it starts moving, you start giving them tidbit you ask them the question, then you go phone, you give tip, ask them, in other words, I don't want to get into the particular now because this is by the way, there's an infinite number of variations, infinite. There's an infinite number of ways the sale can unfold, but yet every sale is the same because at the end of the day, you're lining up these elements. The straight line is your, this is your, that once in a while, I think this is where I can maybe confuse people sometimes by saying, it's that everyone's on the perfect lay down sale, it's not just that, it's also the perfect sale that could have three or four objections, but by nature of how they answered your questions and their temperament, their personality, everything on your script that you wrote out was how you moved them from the open to the close. That is gonna be exceedingly rare. In the real world of straight line selling, the script is like your checklist, checklist. So if you're forced to jump forward, your mind says, okay, I know I have an open loop here at a certain point where it makes sense and it's elegant, I'm gonna loop back, take this box, if it needs to be ticked, ticked, ticked, ticked, but very often what happens is, is someone will say something and they'll take three boxes by one statement, but yet because you didn't directly ask that question, you think you're still stuck back on this, you're not. You move forward on the line. The reason the straight line is so effective is because you end up figuring this, you all figure this out on your own. It doesn't take you that long because you're saying, what's the way, you have to free form, but my goal with this train is to cut that period in half. Okay, so Ed, when you first start understand, you're not robots, you're not Siri. Siri can't take the, yeah, and probably another year or two will be different, right? She, you ask her one question, maybe they have it now so they can, you can almost ask a second one if it's directly related, but after that, it's not like the knowledge that the AI gained from what you respond to served to essentially serve this individual, no, it's done, it's lost into the ether, you're back to square zero, but not you, you're not a computer, you're far smarter than that. And the AI's are not up to human standards yet, one day they will be. Now they're not able, these these, to take context and apply it to the future in a sales setting. They can't do it yet. They can do it like in chess and certain things in Go because they've really worked at that many permutations, but you can, you can listen to the way a client responds and know how fast or slow you should be moving down the line based on are they speaking slowly, they speaking quickly. When you asked them the first question, if they, I was the call, someone guys said, what do you know I'm calling about about Jordan, but oh my God, Jordan, but I love the guy. How could you not love this stuff? It's unbelievable. It's great to sell the guy the certification program. Guy and the guys, yeah, I ran a sale, I run a sales force of 20 corporate selling, but he's fucking qualified. What is the point of trying to find out all the financial, tell me all I want is great. What are you selling? What are the big problems you're facing right now in selling what if there's some things we can improve on, which aspects of the straight line are you using? Have you bought any products? You just, what you've gotten online? Great, well, let me say this based on what you said. This is the end what the guy else said. Oh yeah, my big problem is I want to go to his events. I'm too busy. I can't take the time or for work to go see him live. And I really want to get to the next level with this stuff. It's like, what are you doing here? Like you need, okay, so tell me now. So tell me how long you've been selling for long enough. You don't need to ask that. Okay. You know what I'm saying? What's your, you don't need to know what to call it. I came and take the pain. I felt listening to my own person sell. I was like, oh my God. And shame on me, I just be too busy. I'm not magic, I'm having to stop just beginning now and manage my own sales force. Cause I was developing this program for you guys. But I saw how even in my own company without the proper training, you people believe you have to follow your script. Your script is your guideline. If at any moment in time, the realities of the actual encounter would cause the script to be even the 1% wooden or not make sense, abandon ship, not abandon script, abandon, that's when you move off the straight line and keep the sale moving inside the boundaries. You're a human. You can do this. You're not Siri. You're not Alexa. You can easily, and this is why it's so important, why I want you to memorize and know this word for word. See, if you know your script and you know now, see what, cause what's your script constructed of? Nuggets, mills, nuggets, I throughout that. This whole thing here, it was made up of 150 nuggets. I want you to know this by heart. I want you to be able, I want you to know it in order and each piece individually, because when you know that, you can abandon the straight line and happily exist in these boundaries here as you present and gather intelligence. And that is when you sound great. That is when you're elegant. When you're trying to stay directly on the straight line, 90% of the time you sound like a fool. You sound like you're reading from a script. You get it? So you're following, you're not abandoning the script. You're just aware that the script is like, it's like a Lego puzzle. Each piece can be taken out and put back here interchangeably, right? The beauty of the system is it allows you to have, cause you know, okay, say I know, I gotta tick these five boxes. I know, must, all those musts represent, that's your framework, that's your boundaries for the sales process. So again, when you're beginning a phone call, what does your script represent? Asks, what is it? Your script represents your 15, 20 best presentations, best language patterns, best everything, all wrapped up into one best guess for that most, what's the most, what's the average, the median client, the mean client, right here that you hear, what's that conversation gonna sound like? What's my best guess on what the conversation will sound like? That is the straight line. Then you have boundaries above and below the line. Those boundaries represent two things. A, the boundaries for going off to Pluto, so you don't get caught up in trying to be an expert by thinking that if they love duck hunting, you should love duck hunting. If they like sword fighting, you like sword fighting, okay, whatever they may travel, you'll be like, oh my God, I'll have to travel. That's part of what the boundaries are. The other thing they are, is they represent the infinite number of variations of that script that can all be used and still be awesome, still be elegant. And accomplish what you want to accomplish. But it's not a word for word match to this one conversation, that's your best guess. When that conversation happens, it happens, yeah, it's great, it's very easy, and you sound awesome. But because you know it's made up of all these nuggets, so this perfect trips made up of nuggets, right? What are you doing? When you're going off the straight line, but staying inside the boundaries, you're deconstructing and moving nuggets around and reforming them, but at the end of the day, it looks like the same exact picture. It's the same picture you form. You drew the same figure, you got to the same place, but you did one thing first, different thing second, and that's how you get to this point where you're closing anyone who's closeable and you sound great. It's a testament to the power of the straight line of how many of you are using this incorrectly right now and still getting great results. But when you get this, it's an understanding. It's a fundamental understanding of what is really happening when you influence someone and what is not happening. Let me just restate this one more time, just to be clear. What's not happening is you are not trying to shove the prospect onto the straight line. That's what you're not doing. You're not trying to take it and ring the, you're not trying to reign in the prospect onto the straight line. What you're doing is reining him inside the boundaries. You're actually adapting the straight line to fit the prospect. So you can go up, down, that's your wiggle room, and as long as you hit the fundamental points. Now by the way, what I mean by hit means, if let's say there's six, let's say there's four things or five things that you need to know to decide whether or not it really even makes sense to move, I mean, when I say move the prospect down the straight line, meaning to keep presenting, to actually present, right? There's four or five things you need to know, and you have eight or nine questions to get to the bottom. If by question number two, I know everything, I'm not belaboring the point because, well, I must ask all my intelligence gathering questions in this order, you sound like a fool, and you're bore people. And you know what you wear them down, and I tell you what happened with this one sale. The guy extended out the intelligence gathering, which is nonsense, of asking questions that didn't matter, and by the way, this guy was really trying to move the sale forward. So it got to the point, he would give this really succinct, but so tell me, what am I gonna do to, how does it work? He was like giving me this like, just tell me how, what is the cost, how does it work? You know? If you spend 20 minutes wearing that guy down by forcing him to go back into intelligence, guess what? He's not gonna stick around for rebuttals and overcoming objections. He's done, turn it off, he's worn down. I'm not saying you can never close, what's gonna take another call. Guy like that could have been qualified in 34 seconds, and you'd be presenting straight away and filling in the blanks. You feel, watch the dairy, the dairy, watch how I keep filling in blanks as I present, and I'm never out of control once. I'm in control the entire time, but I'm asking questions, I'm answering their questions, I'm presenting, and I'm moving and grooving here forward back, but always within the boundaries. That's what these boundaries represent as much as boundaries to Pluto. That I'm gonna say it one more time, have a bone cut here, I'm gonna cut it here today. Have a bone cut, right? I'm gonna say it one more time, yeah, I'm gonna start cutting it here, you'll be guys enough time for that. Again, what this represents is the infinite number of ways you can adjust the script to make this presentation perfectly match what the client needs to say, so what would J.B. do? How would he handle it? How would he move and groove around the straight line to make sure this person, bam, he's sharp, and think of it that way. I don't stick to my script. You know, at the John Maxwell train, one of the guys, the top guy, they say, it's amazing now that I really understand what you're doing, it's not like scripting, you need to do dynamic scripting. It's dynamic presenting, in other words, your script is your best estimate of what's gonna happen, and your dynamic presentation is, woo, you're going around that, and when you see it happen, it's beautiful. It really is, it's beautiful to watch, it sounds good, and people respond, and that's what the reason I originally said, it ends up clearing itself up, what a map, and it does, because when you get really good at this, your unconscious mind takes over and automatically does this, because it knows. Hey, Jordan and Bo, I posted a question last week about the Assumptive Clothes. I think the way that I asked the question made it hard to actually identify what I was really asking for. Yeah, I remember, yeah, I was like, exactly, yeah. Just to be clear, I'm wondering why the Assumptive Clothes isn't more prevalent in your program. It seems to be a vital impact on our closing rates and I feel like a lot of our sales training dance around the style of closing. Now, that being said, I really want to break this down so that there's no confusion. I'm saying that there's a big difference in asking for the sale rather than telling the customer what you're going to do for them and then letting them agree with the decision that you are making for them. In my industry, this is referred to as being an order taker or an order creator. Order takers are asking customers to buy their product and hoping that they will make a decision to purchase the product. Order creators are telling the customer that they are going to send them the product and then letting the customer agree or disagree with the decision that the sales has already made for them. He says, one more paragraph. The way I explained this last week was taking the burden of decision making off the customer's shoulders and I like explaining it that way because it's easier for new sales reps to grasp what I mean. Well, I would say, I would not even dive into how much you're gonna charge them, say, obviously, if it gets, whatever your script is, okay, I focus on other things outside of directly saying, commission what you're gonna charge them to list their home, say, listen, if someone says, what are you gonna charge them? I'll be there both Tuesday and Wednesday, which day is better for you? I don't want you to be unethical, why? But if you're not, it sounds like you're not looking to close anybody, you just wanna get to their house to meet them in person, right? So, you know, don't focus on, you know, say, listen, obviously, whatever they charge you, listen, one thing about us, you know, we make it up a point, okay, not only to get the home sold as quickly as possible, but at a higher price, okay, but of course, okay, we always have, you know, a lot of wiggle room on what we charge, most employee, I'm actually gonna be in your area in the next, you know, next Tuesday, give them two days, okay? Happy to come by and discuss all this to you in person, which day's better for you, Tuesday, Wednesday? If that's what you're doing here, don't be getting locked down, and what's your commission rate on a sale that you haven't even had as a client yet. I think you'll do a lot better getting to the home before that and making that case in front of them, you can show them numbers and testimonials. You charge someone 3%, and someone else charges 2%, but they get a low ball, and when a shit job's selling in the house, it'll take extra four weeks or six weeks, and they'll get a low price. I'd rather go with the guy charge me a little bit more, but you're gonna have a lot more success making that case face-to-face on a second, on a follow-up when you're in person, so just don't get caught up in the weeds. That's very obvious to me. So, what I was saying to the maximum, it was like, you know, you have these sort of anchor points that you, so you can free form, and when I say, you know, you always loop back to your anchor, like at a certain point, I gotta be here, I gotta be here, I gotta be here. You have your landing spots on the straight line, right? So, when you are in here, and you're off the straight line inside the boundary, remember what anchor point you need to get back to. When you start presenting, making your first feature benefit is an anchor point. So, you can actually free form around those, but you always know, as you're talking, you're ending in, so what we do with anchor points is it's so important, and this is another thing that Chris is driving at here, is when I said to Maxwell team, I said, guys, you especially need to know the beginning, the first sentence, and the, let's say the last one, by rope, like by, you know, you gotta know it really well, the first and last sentence of each pattern, the first four words and last four words of each pattern, because what you want here is, as you're in that last sentence of one pattern, you're unconscious mind is taking over it, because you know it by heart. So, what that does, it frees you up to start double tracking. So, you're talking now, but it's out of memory. So, you think, okay, where am I going next? Where's my next anchor point? And that allows you to go to the next anchor point, and each anchor point, you have the first sentence memorized. So, the first four or five words. So, you end and begin each pattern with a memorized little chunk of info. So, when you start to approach the end of, when you're landing on one pattern, your unconscious mind fully kicks in, and speaking to the client automatically, your conscious mind is saying, where am I going now? Where am I going now? Oh, I gotta go to the pattern, I gotta go to the, I gotta go back to this point of straight line, and when you land there, your unconscious mind can kick in there too, because you know the first two words by heart, or four words by heart too. It allows you to sound perfect. You don't need to have every single word of your script memorized, but you have these beginning and ends to each pattern locked down in your mind. In the middle, you can dodge and weave, and sound good. But by doing that, you're using the power of the unconscious mind, which is with this infinite storage capacity, to allow you to, like, you're talking to the prospect, and you sound the great, and your conscious mind is completely not even, doesn't even know what you're saying, it's just automatic, and you think, okay, I gotta go back to here right now, and then you go back to there. When you get there, your unconscious mind already is, your unconscious mind is kicked back in again, right?
